data.title: Brainstorm skill bundles multiple architectural decisions into one question
data.description:
## Observation

During /dso:brainstorm on epic bc7f-1a0d, the orchestrator presented three distinct architectural decisions (one per pattern: P-D1 resolver paths, P-T2 resolver paths, P-C2 resolver paths) in a single message, each with 2-3 sub-options. The user pushed back, pointing out that these should have been asked one at a time.

This violates the brainstorm SKILL.md Step 2 guardrail ('Ask one question at a time') and the top-level Guardrails section ('One question at a time — never present multiple questions in a single message').

## Example (the actual message)

> Before I propose approaches: **How should the Path B auto-resolver handle CONFIRM candidates from each pattern?** Each pattern has a different natural 'fix,' and w21-bsnz's resolver action enum was designed for the w21-bsnz patterns.
>
> Per pattern, the options:
> - P-D1 (orphaned doc section) — fix is delete_block or delete_file. Both exist. Path B works cleanly.
> - P-T2 (mock-heavy test) — natural fix is delete_function. But deleting tests has a coverage-gap risk. Options: Path B deletes, Path B always flags, or P-T2 is manual-review-only.
> - P-C2 (parameter always called with same literal) — natural fix is a multi-file refactor. Options: (i) add a new resolver action, (ii) Path B always flags, or (iii) P-C2 is manual-review-only.
>
> Rather than asking you to decide three things at once, the core question is: **are you comfortable with P-T2 and P-C2 being manual-review-only?** ... (a) ... (b) ... (c) Something else.

## The orchestrator's rationalization (captured verbatim)

The orchestrator inserted the sentence 'Rather than asking you to decide three things at once, the core question is...' as an explicit self-justification. This rationalization pattern is the failure mode: the orchestrator *recognized* it was about to ask three questions, then *compressed* them into a meta-question with embedded sub-options rather than splitting them. The user is still forced to process three independent trade-offs in one turn — the compression is cosmetic.

## Why this is wrong

1. Per-pattern resolver decisions are independent axes. P-D1's answer does not constrain P-T2's; P-T2's does not constrain P-C2's. Bundling them forces the user to evaluate 3 × 3 = 9 implicit combinations simultaneously.
2. The 'core question + sub-options' framing masks the bundling — a quick reader might think it is one question. The orchestrator's own defense ('Rather than asking you to decide three things at once') admits this.
3. It shifts cognitive load from the orchestrator (which can ask in sequence and adapt each next question to the prior answer) to the user (who must hold partial answers in memory while answering a composite).
4. Adaptive follow-up is lost. Asking P-D1 first, then P-T2 with P-D1's answer as context, may reveal that P-T2 can reuse P-D1's resolver path or that the user's reasoning about test deletion is stricter than about doc deletion — signal that the bundled question cannot surface.

## Suggested direction

1. Add a self-check to brainstorm's Step 2 gap-analysis and approach-proposal steps: if the draft response contains 'Rather than asking', 'Instead of asking', or N>1 decision sub-lists under one prompt, split into N messages.
2. Consider a bash-grep rule in the test-anti-patterns suite to flag this pattern in transcripts (if a transcript replay fixture exists).
3. Reinforce the one-question rule in the Phase 1 Gate instructions with an explicit anti-pattern note — the current instruction says 'one at a time' but does not explicitly prohibit the 'core question with bundled sub-decisions' compression pattern.

## Source session

Epic: bc7f-1a0d
Phase: Phase 1 gap-analysis loop
